content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 9-814
Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901            322-6441           322-6444

June 21, 1979

Dear Cromwell
Executive Officer
State Board of Forestry
1416 Ninth Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Cromwell:

This is in response to your inquiry on behalf of Virginia
Harwood, a member of the State Board of Forestry, concerning
her ability to participate in the Board's decisions on proposed
amendments to the Forest Practice Rules for the Southern Sub-
district. This advice is provided pursuant to Section 83114(b)
of the Political Reform Act.1/ The facts as I understand them
from our conversations are as follows.

Ms. Harwood has an investment worth more than $1000 in a
company which owns approximately 1500 acres of timberland in
the Southern Subdistrict which the company manages under
established timber harvest plans. In addition, the company
conducts timber operations on a significant amount of acreage
in the subdistrict which it does not own but which it manages
under contract with the owners. The proposed amendments to the
Forest Practice Rules for the Southern Subdistrict will open
up new areas and will make notable changes in the methods of
operation which the timber companies operating in the area
are allowed to use.

Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act operate together to
require Ms. Harwood to disqualify herself from participating
in any decision of the State Board of Forestry which will have

1/ All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

Dean Cromwell
June 21, 1979                                                    Page Two


a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on this company. By regulation the Commission has determined that the phrase "public generally" means a
significant segment of the public generally and that:

     An industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public if the statute, ordinance
or other provision of law which creates or authorizes
the creation of the official's agency or office contains a finding and declaration, including an express
reference to Section 87103 of the Government Code,
to the following effect:

          The Legislature [or other authority]
          declares that the individual[s] appointed
          to the office of _________ is [are]
          intended to represent and further the
          interest of the [specified industry, trade
          or profession], and that such representation and furtherance will ultimately serve
          the public interest. Accordingly, the
          Legislature [or other authority] finds
          that for purposes of persons who hold such
          office the [specified industry, trade or
          profession] is tantamount to and constitutes the public generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of the Government
          Code.

                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703(c)

     The Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practices Act, has recently been
amended by the Legislature to include such a specific declaration
with respect to those members of the State Board of Forestry who
must be chosen from the forest products and range livestock
industries. See Public Resources Code Section 731.1. Consequently, those industries do constitute a significant segment
of the public within the meaning of the Political Reform Act,
and Ms. Harwood is not required to disqualify herself from any
decision of the Board which will affect the company in which
she has an investment unless the decision will peculiarly and
directly affect the company in a manner different from its
effect on all other members of the industry. See the opinion
requested by James F. Callanan, 4 FPPC Ops. 33, 37 (No. 76-036,
1978).

     In the present circumstances, the proposed amendments to
the Forest Practices Rules for the Southern Subdistrict will
have a similar effect on all of the several companies operating

Dean Cromwell
June 21, 1979

Page Three

in the area. There is no reason to believe that the company in
which Ms. Harwood has an investment will be peculiarly or directly
affected by the amendments. She may therefore participate in
the Board's consideration of those amendments without violating
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.

If you have any further questions on this matter, please
do not hesitate to call me.

Sincerely,


Sarah T. Cameron
Counsel
Conflicts of Interest Division

STC:mfa

RESOURCES AGENCY                                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR.
                                                                             GOVERNOR


                              State of California

                         Board of Forestry
                              1416 NINTH STREET
                         SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814


TO:      Mr. Ted Prim                                    March 21, 1979
         Fair Political Practices Commission

FROM:    Dean Cromwell, Executive Officer

SUBJECT: CONFLICT OF INTEREST ADVICE


The issue of potential conflict of interest has been raised
regarding participation of Board Member Virginia Harwood in voting
on proposed forest practice rules for the Southern Subdistrict
of the Coast Forest District. Information relative to the
mandates of the Board, its composition, and the proposed rules
have already been sent to your office.

Mrs. Harwood is joint owner of Harwood Forest Products. This
company owns about 1490 acres of timberland in Santa Cruz County.
The Company is very active in buying logs in the Southern
Subdistricts. Many management arrangements can be made in
log buying, including preparation of timber harvesting plans
and contract logging. The Harwood's obtain sizable income from
their property in Santa Cruz and spend a significant sum of
money to buy logs from other timber owners.

On behalf of Mrs. Harwood, would you please advise on the follow-
ing two questions:

1. Does she have a foreseeable material financial interest
   as described in FPPC regulations in activities affected by
   the proposal?

2. Despite the fact that she may have a foreseeable material
   financial interest, can she participate because members
   of the forest products industry are specifically authorized
   as members of the Board of Forestry, and thus the forest
   products industry constitutes a significant segment of
   the public within the meaning of FPPC regulations?

As the Board of Forestry meeting is March 27, could we please
have some advice by March 26?


                                        Dean A. Cromwell
                                        Executive Officer

RESOURCES AGENCY                                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR.
                                                                              GOVERNOR




                              State of California

                         Board of Forestry
                              1416 NINTH STREET
                         SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814




TO:     Honorable John Nejedly                          March 20, 1979
        Member of the Senate

FROM:   Dean Cromwell
        Executive Officer

SUBJECT: Bill Draft #5-5832, Clarifying the State Board of
         Forestry Conflict of Interest Provisions Under the
         Fair Political Practices Act


I.  REASONS FOR REQUEST

    On March 9, a serious ambiguity became apparent in the
    Conflict of Interest provisions affecting the Board of
    Forestry. An inquiry filed with the Fair Political
    Practices Commission (FPPC), about a Board Members'
    possible conflict of interest, has raised the possibility
    that industry members will need to frequently disqualify
    themselves from voting on forest practice rules in those
    regions where their company or personal forest holding
    are located. This would have the effect of making the
    Board a five member public Board when voting on many
    forest practice rules.

    Because March 9 was also the last day to submit bill drafting
    requests, because of the possible urgency of the impact on
    the Board, and at the advice of the Fair Political Practices
    Commission staff, a request was made to your office to
    draft a bill to clarify matters. The bill, as proposed,
    would set forth legislative intent consistent with regula-
    tions of the Fair Political Practices Commission (2 CAC 18703(c)),
    and clearly permit industry members to vote on most forest
    practice issues.

II. BACKGROUND

    On March 8, Board of Forestry staff was notified by the FPPC
    Conflict of Interest Division, that they had received a
    complaint about possible conflict of interest of one of
    the Board's industry representatives when participating in
    discussion and voting on forest practice regulations that
    affect San Mateo and Santa Cruz Counties. The member
    involved is part owner of a company which buys timber and
    owns land in Santa Cruz County.

Honorable John Nejedly
March 20, 1979
Page 2

The Board of Forestry is considering regulations that
will possibly increase harvest levels in Santa Cruz
County. Inasmuch as changes in forest practice rules
could affect the income of timber companies doing business
in Santa Cruz County, including the Board members' company,
questions were raised about possible conflict of interest
provisions under the Political Reform Act.

Based on discussions with the FPPC Conflict of Interest
Division staff, the problem comes from the conflict of
interest provisions which go counter to the intent of
the language of the Board's enabling statute.

Government Code Section 87100 provides that, "no public
official....shall make, participate in making, or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest."

Government Code Section 87103 provides that an official
has "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it
is "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a
material effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on a source of an income, an investment,
or an interest in real property.

By regulation, the Fair Political Practices Commission
has defined "material" in numerical terms (2 CAC 18702).
They have also said that a material financial effect is
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
unless the decision will affect the official's interest
in substantially the same manner as it will affect all
members of the public, or a significant segment of the
general public (2 CAC 18703).

Since its passage in 1974, the language in the Government
Code has raised a number of problems for Boards and
Commissions who statutorily have as members, representa-
tives for the industries which they advise or regulate.
Operations of regulatory or advisory commissions would
be adversely affected if industry members had to disqualify.

For example, PRC 731 creates a nine member State Board
of Forestry which includes five members selected from the
general public, three members from the forest products
industry, and one from the range-livestock industry. These
members shall all "represent the general public interest."
(PRC 736.5 requires Board members to disqualify themselves
if they have a "direct personal financial interest within
the meaning of Section 1120 of the Government Code" in a
decision.) However, Section 1120 has been repealed and
replaced by the Fair Political Practice Act.

Honorable John Nejedly
March 20, 1979
Page 3

To deal with the problem, the Fair Political Practices
Commission, in 1976, ruled that through January 1, 1979,
an industry constituted a significant segment of the
public if the agency's enabling statute expressly required
that members of the industry hold office.

This rule was sustained by the courts in litigation and
was applied to the Board of Forestry along with various
consumer boards. Consequently, industry members voted
on all forest practice rules, even if they or their firms
had holdings in the forest practice district involved.

However, the same rule states that after January 1979, an
industry may constitute a significant segment of the
public only if one of two conditions exists (1) if there
are express legislative findings declaring that persons
are intended to represent and further the interest of the
industry, and thus the public; or (2) it is implicit in
the enabling statutes (2 CAC 18703(d)), that industry
members are on the Board to represent and further the
interests of the industry, and therefore the public.

The Board of Forestry overlooked this January 1979 cutoff
date and was reminded of it when responding to the inquiry
on March 9 from the FPPC Conflict of Interest Division.
FPPC staff has said that the Board of Forestry has two
options: seek explicit legislative findings to resolve the
issue; or request an opinion of the FPPC that interprets
the enabling statutes of the Board. They advise that the
preferred way is to seek legislative clarifications.

Pending either legislative findings or action by the
Commission itself, the FPPC staff states that its interim
advice to the Board member concerned will be to disqualify
from participating in forest practice rule discussions
if they have a foreseeable material financial interest.
Until the matter is definitely solved, this issue,
logically extended, could lead to the disqualification
of all industry representatives when a forest practice
rule impacts their own harvesting operations.

This narrow interpretation seems to go counter to the
statement in the Board's enabling statute that all members
are to represent the general public interest.

The bill with which the Board has requested the assistance
of your office is modeled after similar legislation
passed in 1976 to protect agricultural commodity boards
and closely follows language suggested by the Fair
Political Practices Commission (2 CAC 18703(c)). It
would resolve the apparent conflict by making it clear that
the industry members are contributing to the general
public interest.

Honorable John Nejedly
March 20, 1979
Page 4


The Board is also exploring administrative remedies but
currently believes that legislative findings are the most
effective way to proceed.


Dean A. Cromwell
Dean A. Cromwell
Executive Officer

sections.question: Does Ms. Harwood have a foreseeable material financial interest as described in FPPC regulations in activities affected by the proposal? Despite the fact that she may have a foreseeable material financial interest, can she participate because members of the forest products industry are specifically authorized as members of the Board of Forestry, and thus the forest products industry constitutes a significant segment of the public within the meaning of FPPC regulations?
sections.conclusion: In the present circumstances, the proposed amendments to the Forest Practices Rules for the Southern Subdistrict will have a similar effect on all of the several companies operating in the area. There is no reason to believe that the company in which Ms. Harwood has an investment will be peculiarly or directly affected by the amendments. She may therefore participate in the Board's consideration of those amendments without violating the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Virginia Harwood, a State Board of Forestry member with investment in a timber company, must disqualify herself from voting on proposed Forest Practice Rules amendments that would affect the timber industry, or whether the forest products industry constitutes a significant segment of the public allowing her participation.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ms. Harwood may participate in voting on the proposed Forest Practice Rules amendments because the forest products industry constitutes a significant segment of the public under the Political Reform Act, and the amendments will affect all timber companies in the area similarly rather than having a peculiar and direct effect on her company.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters including incoming request from Board of Forestry (March 21, 1979) and FPPC response letter (June 21, 1979). The response letter is the primary advice letter; incoming request and legislative correspondence included for context. Analysis based on FPPC counsel's response and application of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Ms. Harwood have a foreseeable material financial interest as described in FPPC regulations in activities affected by the proposal? Despite the fact that she may have a foreseeable material financial interest, can she participate because members of the forest products industry are specifically authorized as members of the Board of Forestry, and thus the forest products industry constitutes a significant segment of the public within the meaning of FPPC regulations?

CONCLUSION: In the present circumstances, the proposed amendments to the Forest Practices Rules for the Southern Subdistrict will have a similar effect on all of the several companies operating in the area. There is no reason to believe that the company in which Ms. Harwood has an investment will be peculiarly or directly affected by the amendments. She may therefore participate in the Board's consideration of those amendments without violating the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC counsel advises that Virginia Harwood, a State Board of Forestry member with investment in a timber company, may participate in voting on proposed Forest Practice Rules amendments for the Southern Subdistrict because the amendments will have a similar effect on all timber companies in the area and the forest products industry qualifies as a significant segment of the public under the Political Reform Act.
